dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to EMS for a reported overdose, unconscious and not breathing, at an apartment building.
We have a male patient being brought out by fire. He appears unresponsive and not breathing.
Chief complaint is suspected overdose with apnea and unresponsiveness.
No eye opening.
No verbal response.
No motor response.
Airway is obstructed by the tongue.
Patient is apneic.
Initial oxygen saturation is 14 percent.
Radial pulse is slow and bounding. Skin is cyanotic, cold, and diaphoretic.
Pupils are pinpoint, about 1 millimeter.
Begin bag-valve-mask ventilations with 15 liters of oxygen.
Nasopharyngeal airway inserted successfully.
Oropharyngeal airway inserted successfully.
Four-lead monitor is on. Rhythm is normal sinus.
ETCO2 is greater than 100 with an apneic waveform, severely hypercapneic.
Establishing an 18-gauge IV in the left antecubital.
Naloxone 1 milligram IV administered.
Patient is waking up and resuming spontaneous respirations.
Airway is now patent.
Breathing is now present with normal rate, rhythm, depth, and effort.
Radial pulses are now rapid, strong, equal, and regular. Skin is warm, dry, and normal in color.
Eyes are now open spontaneously.
Patient is answering questions appropriately and is oriented.
Patient is moving all extremities and following commands.
Hey, you're with EMS. You stopped breathing, but we gave you naloxone. Can you tell me what you took today?
I used IV heroin today.
Did you use more than usual, or anything else like alcohol or other drugs?
No other drugs or alcohol today. I didn't take more than normal, but it may have been a stronger batch or maybe I mismeasured it.
Do you have a history of heroin use?
Yes. I've abused heroin for about eight years and used it intravenously for six years. I usually use about once a week.
Any medications or allergies?
No medications, and I don't have any known drug allergies.
Any chest pain, shortness of breath, fever, chills, cough, abdominal pain, head, neck, or back pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or vision changes?
No. I don't have any of those, and I haven't been around anyone with COVID either.
Head is normocephalic. Pupils are equal, round, and reactive at 2 millimeters. No jugular venous distention and no trauma noted.
Chest rise is normal. Breath sounds are clear and equal bilaterally. No chest wall tenderness or discoloration.
Abdomen is soft, without tenderness or discoloration.
Positive pulse, motor, and sensation in both arms and legs with normal strength and coordination. No swelling, edema, or other trauma noted.
Blood pressure is 116 over 77.
Pulse is 70.
Respirations are now 16 and regular.
Repeat four-lead still shows normal sinus rhythm.
ETCO2 waveform is now normal with normocapnia.
We're moving you from the fourth floor on the stair chair, then onto the stretcher.
Patient secured on stretcher in semi-Fowler's position with shoulder and lap belts.
Vitals and assessment rechecked in the ambulance. Patient remains stable.
Do you understand that the heroin caused you to stop breathing and that the naloxone reversed it?
Yes, I understand.
We're transporting you to VCU Health Systems.
Hospital contacted with pre-arrival report for opioid overdose with respiratory failure reversed by naloxone.
Continuing overdose and opioid protocol management during transport and reassessment.

Bedside report given to hospital staff. Thirty-five-year-old male found apneic, cyanotic, and unresponsive with suspected opioid overdose, ventilated with BVM and airway adjuncts, received 1 milligram IV naloxone with improvement to alert and oriented status, then transported in stable condition.
